children.name: Passer
children.description: Intimately associated with human settlement for thousands of years. The House Sparrow has declined catastrophically in western European cities since the 1990s, including Stockholm, probably due to loss of nest sites in modernised buildings and reduced invertebrate food for chicks. The Tree Sparrow is slightly more rural, identified by its chestnut crown and black ear spot.

children.name: Passer domesticus
children.description: The familiar gråsparv of farmyards and towns, intimately tied to human settlement for millennia, but in sharp decline in many European cities including Stockholm. Males have a grey crown and black bib.

children.name: Passer montanus
children.description: A neater, more rural counterpart to the House Sparrow, both sexes alike with a chocolate-brown crown and a black spot on a white cheek. It favours farmland, orchards, and village edges across Sweden.

children.name: Passer ammodendri
children.description: Inhabits Central Asian desert scrub dominated by saxaul shrubs. It has a pale sandy-brown plumage with a distinctive black throat patch and a long tail. Feeds mostly on seeds and insects in arid riparian zones.

children.name: Passer castanopterus
children.description: A small sparrow endemic to the Horn of Africa, with a chestnut wing patch and pale underparts. It inhabits dry savanna and scrubland, often near water sources. Its range extends from Djibouti and Somalia into eastern Ethiopia.

children.name: Passer cordofanicus
children.description: A grey-headed sparrow found in South Sudan and Chad, formerly considered conspecific with the Great Sparrow. It has a pale grey crown and nape with a warm brown back. Its range centres on the Kordofan region of Sudan.

children.name: Passer diffusus
children.description: A pale grey-headed sparrow of southern Africa, from Angola and Zambia south to South Africa. It has a soft grey crown, whitish cheeks, and a warm brown back. It favours open woodland, farmland, and suburban gardens.

children.name: Passer eminibey
children.description: One of the smallest Passer species, the male is a striking deep chestnut-red year-round. It occurs from Sudan and Ethiopia south to Tanzania, inhabiting dry savanna and acacia scrub. Often seen in small flocks foraging for grass seeds.

children.name: Passer euchlorus
children.description: A brilliant golden-yellow sparrow found in southwestern Arabia. Males are an unmistakable rich gold with pale bills, while females are duller. It inhabits arid thorn scrub and wadis, often near human habitation.

children.name: Passer flaveolus
children.description: A small, relatively unmarked sparrow of Southeast Asia, from Myanmar to Vietnam and south to the Malay Peninsula. It has a plain brown back and pale underparts, with males showing a black bib. It favours open lowlands and cultivated areas.

children.name: Passer gongonensis
children.description: The largest Passer species, reaching 18 cm with a massive pale conical bill reminiscent of a parrot. It ranges from South Sudan and Ethiopia through Kenya to Tanzania. It inhabits dry savanna and brushland.

children.name: Passer griseus
children.description: A common sparrow across tropical Africa, with a pale grey head and throat contrasting with a brown back. It is highly adaptable and occurs in savanna, farmland, and towns from Senegal to Ethiopia and south to Angola. It often nests in buildings and streetlights.

children.name: Passer hemileucus
children.description: A pale, sandy-coloured sparrow endemic to the island of Abd al-Kuri in the Socotra archipelago. It has a distinctive white belly and pale grey head. Its restricted range and small population make it vulnerable to habitat degradation.

children.name: Passer hispaniolensis
children.description: A stocky sparrow with a heavy black breast patch and bold white cheek patches in breeding males. It ranges from the Mediterranean and North Africa through the Middle East to Central Asia. It forms large, noisy colonies in trees and reedbeds.

children.name: Passer iagoensis
children.description: A small sparrow endemic to the Cape Verde Islands, where it inhabits arid rocky slopes and towns. Males have a rich chestnut back and black bib. It is named after the island of Santiago (Iago).

children.name: Passer insularis
children.description: A pale grey-brown sparrow restricted to Socotra Island in the Indian Ocean. It has a distinctive grey head and white supercilium. It is common across the island's arid plateaus and settled areas.

children.name: Passer italiae
children.description: Endemic to Italy and parts of the Mediterranean, this sparrow has a chestnut crown like the Tree Sparrow but the grey cheeks and black bib of the House Sparrow. Its taxonomic status has been debated as a possible hybrid-derived species. It is common across Italy and Corsica.

children.name: Passer luteus
children.description: A vivid golden-yellow sparrow of the Sahel zone from Senegal to Sudan. Males are brilliant yellow with pale bills and a faint chestnut wash on the back. It forms enormous nomadic flocks in acacia savanna.

children.name: Passer melanurus
children.description: The male has a striking black-and-white head pattern with a chestnut nape, making it one of the most handsome sparrows. It is common across southern Africa from Angola to South Africa, favouring arid savanna and farmlands. Often seen in pairs or small groups.

children.name: Passer moabiticus
children.description: A small, pale sparrow found around the Dead Sea basin and east to Iran and Afghanistan. It has a distinctive yellow throat patch and pale grey head. It nests in colonies in tamarisk and other desert shrubs.

children.name: Passer motitensis
children.description: A large, robust sparrow of southern Africa with a black bill and a grey crown. It has a rich chestnut back and bold white wing bars. It inhabits dry savanna and mopane woodland in Angola, Namibia, Botswana, Zimbabwe, and northern South Africa.

children.name: Passer pyrrhonotus
children.description: A small, nondescript sparrow restricted to the Indus Valley region of Pakistan and northwestern India. It resembles the House Sparrow but is slightly smaller with a chestnut nape. It favours riverine thickets, especially acacia and tamarisk.

children.name: Passer rufocinctus
children.description: A grey-headed sparrow found in the highlands of Kenya and Tanzania. It has a rufous collar and chestnut back with a pale grey crown. It inhabits open woodland, farmlands, and suburban gardens.

children.name: Passer cinnamomeus
children.description: A richly coloured sparrow with a cinnamon-brown back and grey head, found from the Himalayas through China to Japan and Korea. In summer males are a bright russet above and yellow below. It inhabits open montane forests and villages.

children.name: Passer shelleyi
children.description: A large grey-headed sparrow of eastern Africa, ranging from South Sudan and Ethiopia to northern Uganda and Kenya. It has a pale grey crown, chestnut mantle, and bold white wing bars. It is named after the British ornithologist George Ernest Shelley.

children.name: Passer simplex
children.description: A pale, ghostly sparrow of the Sahara, with sandy-grey plumage that blends into desert landscapes. It has a faint yellow throat spot and a pale bill. It inhabits oases and dry wadis, often far from human settlement.

children.name: Passer suahelicus
children.description: A grey-headed sparrow native to coastal Kenya and Tanzania. It resembles the Northern Grey-headed Sparrow but is slightly smaller with a paler crown. It inhabits dry savanna and coastal scrub.

children.name: Passer swainsonii
children.description: A dark, sooty-brown sparrow of northeastern Africa, from Ethiopia to Somalia and south to Tanzania. It has a dark grey head and a thick black bill. It is named after the English naturalist William Swainson.

children.name: Passer zarudnyi
children.description: A pale desert sparrow of Central Asia, found in Uzbekistan and Turkmenistan. It was formerly treated as a subspecies of the Desert Sparrow but is now recognized as a distinct species. It has sandy-grey plumage with a pale bill.

children.name: Gymnoris
children.description: The bush sparrows or rock sparrows, a group of four Afrotropical and Asian species. They are medium-sized sparrows with relatively long tails and pale bills. Males typically have a yellow throat patch, especially conspicuous during the breeding season.

children.name: Gymnoris pyrgita
children.description: A pale grey-brown sparrow with a small yellow throat spot, found across the Sahel from Senegal to Ethiopia and south to Tanzania. It inhabits dry acacia savanna and thorn scrub. It has a distinctive pale eye-ring.

children.name: Gymnoris superciliaris
children.description: A small sparrow with a bright yellow throat patch and a conspicuous white supercilium. It ranges from Gabon and Angola across southern Africa to Mozambique and South Africa. It favours miombo woodland and dry savanna.

children.name: Gymnoris dentata
children.description: A tiny sparrow of the Sahel belt, from Mauritania and Senegal east to Sudan. It is the smallest member of the genus, with a fine bill and pale sandy plumage. It inhabits dry grassland and sparse scrub.

children.name: Gymnoris xanthocollis
children.description: A small sparrow with a distinct yellow throat patch, found from Turkey and Iraq through Iran to India and Bangladesh. It has a pale grey head and brown back with double wing bars. It is common in open dry forest, cultivation, and villages.

children.name: Petronia
children.description: The Rock Sparrow is the sole member of this genus, a sturdy, streaked bird of rocky hillsides and open stony terrain across southern Europe, North Africa, and Asia. It has a pale bill and a distinctive yellow patch on the breast that is often hard to see.

children.name: Petronia petronia
children.description: A large, streaky brown sparrow with a stout pale bill and a concealed yellow throat patch. It inhabits rocky slopes, quarries, and ruins from the Canary Islands and Iberia across southern Europe and North Africa to Central Asia. It is more terrestrial than most sparrows.

children.name: Carpospiza
children.description: The Pale Rockfinch is the only species in this genus, a pale, finch-like bird of arid rocky hillsides. It has a thin bill compared to other sparrows and a soft, lark-like song. It breeds from Turkey east to Afghanistan and winters in northeastern Africa.

children.name: Carpospiza brachydactyla
children.description: A plain, sandy-grey bird that resembles a lark more than a sparrow, with a delicate pointed bill. It breeds on arid rocky slopes from Turkey to Iran and the Levant, migrating to East Africa for the winter. Its simple warbling song is given in display flight.

children.name: Hypocryptadius
children.description: The Cinnamon Ibon is an enigmatic passerine endemic to the mountains of Mindanao in the Philippines. Long considered a white-eye, molecular studies have firmly placed it within Passeridae as the most basal sparrow lineage. It forages in forest canopies for insects and fruit.

children.name: Hypocryptadius cinnamomeus
children.description: A small, olive-cinnamon bird with a pale eye-ring and a thin, pointed bill. It is restricted to montane forest on Mindanao, Philippines, where it often joins mixed-species foraging flocks. Its placement in Passeridae was confirmed by molecular phylogenetics in 2010.

children.name: Montifringilla
children.description: The snowfinches — sturdy, pale-bodied birds of high-altitude plateaus and mountain slopes. They are well-adapted to cold environments with extensive white in the wings and tail that is flashed in flight. They occur across the high mountains of southern Europe, Central Asia, and the Tibetan Plateau.

children.name: Montifringilla nivalis
children.description: The most widespread snowfinch, found in high mountain ranges from the Pyrenees and Alps through the Caucasus and Himalayas to western China. It has a pale grey head, brown back, and striking white wing patches with black flight feathers. It inhabits rocky alpine zones above the treeline.

children.name: Montifringilla adamsi
children.description: A high-altitude snowfinch of the Tibetan Plateau, with a pale sandy-brown body and extensive black in the wings. It has a thick pale bill and a simple musical call. It is named after Edward Adams, a British naval surgeon and naturalist.

children.name: Montifringilla henrici
children.description: Endemic to the high steppes of Tibet and adjacent Qinghai, this snowfinch resembles the White-winged Snowfinch but is slightly smaller with a darker crown. It inhabits alpine meadows and rocky slopes at elevations above 4000 m. It was named after Prince Henri of Orléans.

children.name: Onychostruthus
children.description: The White-rumped Snowfinch is the sole species in this genus, distinguished from Montifringilla by its white rump and unique tail feather structure. It is a high-altitude specialist of the Tibetan Plateau, well-camouflaged against the rocky scree it inhabits.

children.name: Onychostruthus taczanowskii
children.description: A pale sandy-grey snowfinch with a pure white rump and black tail feathers, found on the Tibetan Plateau. It has a thick yellowish bill and extensive white in the wings. It is named after the Polish ornithologist Władysław Taczanowski.

children.name: Pyrgilauda
children.description: The ground snowfinches — four small, pale snowfinch species that inhabit the high-altitude steppes and rocky slopes of Central Asia and the Tibetan Plateau. They are more terrestrial than Montifringilla and have less white in the wings. Named after the Greek pyrgos (tower) and lauda (lark), reflecting their ground-dwelling habits.

children.name: Pyrgilauda theresae
children.description: A small, pale snowfinch restricted to the high mountains of northeastern Afghanistan and adjacent Tajikistan. It has a sandy-brown body, a pale grey head, and a dark bib. It was named after Theresa, the wife of its discoverer.

children.name: Pyrgilauda ruficollis
children.description: A distinctive snowfinch with a warm rufous band across the neck and upper breast, found on the Tibetan Plateau and in the Himalayas. It has a pale grey head and sandy-brown back. It inhabits alpine meadows and grassy slopes between 3000 and 5000 m.

children.name: Pyrgilauda davidiana
children.description: A small snowfinch of the Tibetan Plateau and Mongolia, with a plain sandy-brown back and a pale grey head. It has a black bib and a white wing flash. It is named after the French missionary and naturalist Père Armand David.

children.name: Pyrgilauda blanfordi
children.description: A pale, sandy snowfinch with a dark brown back and white wing patches, found on the Tibetan Plateau and in the Karakoram. It has a short stout bill and a faint dark bib. It is named after the British zoologist William Thomas Blanford.
